# The Color Triangle

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2025-02-06
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/AC2LsmSRGTE
**Views:** 10,489
**Guest:** Jinjer Markley (illustrator, color theory teacher)

## Description

Interview with Jinjer Markley, creator of the Color Triangle — a tool that replaces the color wheel with a triangle to more accurately predict paint mixtures. CMY at the corners, actual pigments plotted within the space, and an offset neutral point to account for green-side mixing curves.

## Transcript

Let's talk to the creator of the Color Triangle. My name is Jinjer Markley, and I'm a freelance illustrator and artist now, but my background is actually in science.

Are there things that bridged over from science to what you do with color and art? The first class that I taught was "Science of Watercolor." In my mind I can picture how colors are related to each other — you can just draw a line between colors, there's related colors along that line. And I was trying to explain this using a color wheel and it's very frustrating because the color wheel is distorted. And so I realized I was like, this would be much easier if it were just a triangle.

So this is like a kit — it comes with the dry erase marker. There's pale colors, mid-tone colors, and dark colors. This circle is a warp of how color actually relates to each other, and the triangle is closer to that.

Okay, if I'm mixing like cadmium red and yellow, might be going along this edge, so I still have a vibrant color. But if I even go slightly over this way, I'm actually traveling further in. So on a wheel it looks crazy because I'm like almost bending toward the center.

So I've got quinacridone magenta and phthalo green, right? Yeah, and if you draw a straight line between those — this is a weird one because you think pink-green, those should be pretty close to complementary, my brain says they should cancel out, but I'm going to get some maybe these muted purples. Muted — exactly, muted purples and blues.

Thanks for joining me and for talking about the triangle. Take care, bye bye.

## Key Concepts

- **Color wheel distortion** — the circular layout warps the actual visual relationships between colors; mixing paths on a wheel bend confusingly toward center
- **Triangle layout advantages:**
  - CMY (true visual subtractive primaries) at corners
  - Actual pigments plotted within the space
  - Neutral point offset to account for wider mixing curves on the green side
  - Straight lines between colors better predict mixture results
- **Mixing path example:** cadmium red + yellow stays along the edge (vibrant), but slight deviation = traveling inward (losing chroma) — the triangle shows this honestly
- **Quinacridone magenta + phthalo green:** seem complementary but actually produce muted purples/blues, not neutral gray — the triangle correctly predicts this
